myths.myth: Apply butter or toothpaste on burns
myths.fact: Cool the burn under running water for 10-20 minutes. Butter and toothpaste trap heat and increase the risk of infection.

myths.myth: Tilt your head back during a nosebleed
myths.fact: Lean forward and pinch the soft part of your nose for 10-15 minutes. Tilting back causes blood to flow into the throat, which can cause choking or vomiting.

myths.myth: Suck venom out of a snake bite
myths.fact: Never try to suck out venom. Keep calm, immobilize the limb, and get to a hospital with anti-venom immediately. Sucking can introduce bacteria and worsen the wound.

myths.myth: Apply a tourniquet for any bleeding
myths.fact: Tourniquets should only be used as a last resort for life-threatening limb bleeding. Direct pressure with a clean cloth is the first line of treatment.

myths.myth: Put something in a seizing person's mouth to prevent tongue biting
myths.fact: Never put anything in a seizing person's mouth. It is physically impossible to swallow your tongue. Objects in the mouth can break teeth or cause choking.

myths.myth: Rub alcohol on the body to reduce fever
myths.fact: Alcohol rubs can cause the body temperature to drop too rapidly and can be absorbed through the skin, especially in children. Use lukewarm water sponging instead.

myths.myth: Apply ice directly on a sprain or injury
myths.fact: Never apply ice directly to skin. Always wrap ice in a cloth and apply for 20 minutes on, 20 minutes off. Direct ice contact can cause frostbite.

myths.myth: Give water to a choking person
myths.fact: Never give water to someone who is choking. It can push the object further down. Perform the Heimlich maneuver (abdominal thrusts) instead.

myths.myth: CPR can restart a stopped heart
myths.fact: CPR maintains blood flow to vital organs until a defibrillator or advanced medical help arrives. It buys time but does not restart the heart on its own.

myths.myth: You should induce vomiting after swallowing poison
myths.fact: Never induce vomiting unless specifically told by a poison control professional. Some poisons cause more damage coming back up. Call poison control immediately.

myths.myth: Peeing on a jellyfish sting helps relieve pain
myths.fact: Urine can actually worsen the sting by triggering more nematocyst discharge. Rinse with vinegar or salt water, then soak in hot water (not scalding) for 20-45 minutes.

myths.myth: You need to wait 24 hours to report a missing person
myths.fact: There is no waiting period to report a missing person. Contact police immediately, especially for children, elderly, or those with medical conditions.
